title: Cuccidati
subtitle: Sicilian filled fig and date cookies
excerpt: Cuccidati are Sicilian Christmas cookies: a short, buttery pastry wrapped around a filling of dried fruit, nuts, and warm spices, baked until lightly golden, then glazed with a thin icing. The filling is dense and dark, fragrant with orange zest, cinnamon, and cloves. In Sicily they are made with dried figs; this version uses medjool dates, which are widely available and carry the same deep sweetness. They keep for weeks in a tin, which is why they are Christmas food.
sourceNotes: Cuccidati are from Sicily, particularly associated with Palermo, and are made for Christmas and the Feast of St Joseph in March. The name derives from the Italian cuccia (grain porridge) via dialect. The cookie spread with Sicilian emigrants to the United States in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries and became part of Italian-American Christmas traditions. Recipes appear in southern Italian and Sicilian cookbooks from the nineteenth century.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well; the filling keeps for 1 week refrigerated.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight tin for 3 weeks. The flavour improves significantly after 3 to 5 days.

content.text: The pastry is a classic shortcrust enriched with egg and a little sugar; it handles easily and holds its shape when baked. The filling is processed fine enough to spread smoothly but not so fine that it becomes a paste. Roll the pastry thin, cut into strips, pipe or spoon the filling down the centre, and roll the strip around the filling, sealing the seam. Cut into individual pieces at an angle. The thin icing goes on while the cookies are just barely warm.

items.prepNote: juice only, to mix with icing sugar

content.text: Rub 120 g cold butter into 350 g flour and 60 g sugar until it resembles breadcrumbs. Add 2 eggs, 0.5 tsp vanilla, and 0.5 tsp baking powder. Mix to a smooth dough, adding a teaspoon of cold water if needed. Wrap and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Pulse 250 g chopped medjool dates, 100 g raisins, 80 g walnuts, 50 g mixed peel, the zest of 1 orange, 2 tbsp honey, 1 tsp cinnamon, and 0.25 tsp ground cloves in a food processor until the mixture forms a coarse, sticky paste. It should hold together but have visible texture.

Heat the oven to 175 degrees C fan. Line two baking trays. On a lightly floured surface, roll the pastry to about 3 mm thickness. Cut into strips about 8 cm wide. Roll the filling into a rope along the centre of each strip. Fold the pastry over the filling and press the edges to seal. Cut diagonally into pieces about 4 cm long. Place seam-side down on the lined trays. Bake for 16 to 18 minutes until lightly golden. Cool on a rack for 10 minutes.
Mix 100 g icing sugar with enough lemon juice to make a thin, runny icing. Brush or drizzle over the still-warm cookies. Leave to set completely before storing.

items.problem: Filling burst through the pastry during baking
items.solution: The pastry was rolled too thin or the seam was not well sealed. Roll to at least 3 mm and press the seam firmly together along the full length. Place seam-side down on the tray so the weight of the cookie holds the seal.

items.problem: Filling is too dry and crumbly
items.solution: Add another tablespoon of honey and pulse again. The filling should be sticky enough to hold together when squeezed.

items.problem: Pastry is very brittle and cracks when rolling
items.solution: The pastry was not rested long enough. Refrigerate for at least 30 minutes. If it still cracks at the edges, let it sit at room temperature for 5 minutes before rolling — it should be cold but not hard.
